sets.title: Finnish local cases: inside vs surface, place vs motion
sets.description: Finnish local cases replace many English prepositions: inner cases describe being in, coming out of, or going into something; outer cases describe being on/at, coming off/from, or going onto/to something.

pairs.question: Which sentence says the person is located in the city?
pairs.explanation: Kaupungissa uses the inessive -ssa/-ssä for location inside or within a place. Kaupungista uses the elative -sta/-stä for movement out of or from a place.

pairs.question: Which sentence describes motion into the city rather than already being there?
pairs.explanation: Kaupunkiin is illative: the destination is being entered or reached. Kaupungissa is inessive: the location is already the setting.

pairs.question: Which sentence says the book is on the table now?
pairs.explanation: Pöydällä uses the adessive -lla/-llä for a surface or nearby location. Pöydältä uses the ablative -lta/-ltä for movement away from that surface or location.

pairs.question: Which sentence focuses on placing the book onto the table?
pairs.explanation: Pöydälle is allative: motion or transfer toward a surface/location. Pöydällä is adessive: static position on or at that surface/location.

sets.title: Finnish possessive suffixes
sets.description: Finnish can mark the possessor with a suffix on the noun: -mme 'our', -nne 'your (plural)', and -nsa/-nsä 'his/her/their'.

pairs.question: Which sentence says the house belongs to us?
pairs.explanation: Talomme ends in -mme, the first-person plural possessive suffix: our house. Talonne ends in -nne, the second-person plural suffix: your house when speaking to more than one person or formally.

pairs.question: Which form can mean his, her, or their house depending on context?
pairs.explanation: Talonsa uses -nsa/-nsä, a third-person possessive suffix. Finnish uses the same surface form for his, her, and their; the context identifies the possessor.

sets.title: Finnish consonant gradation
sets.description: Many Finnish nouns and verbs change their stem consonants when endings are added, so the inflected form may not look like the dictionary form plus a suffix.

pairs.label_a: weak grade in a case form
pairs.question: Which sentence shows kaupunki with consonant gradation inside a case form?
pairs.explanation: Kaupunki changes to kaupung- before the inessive ending -ssa: kaupungissa. The nk/ng alternation is consonant gradation, not a separate word.

pairs.question: Which sentence contains the inessive form 'in the bank'?
pairs.explanation: Pankki loses one k before -ssa: pankissa. This k/zero alternation is a common gradation pattern in words ending in -kk-.

pairs.question: Which sentence shows the weak-grade stem in a location case?
pairs.explanation: Jalka becomes jalassa in the inessive: the k disappears before -ssa. Recognizing the stem alternation helps connect inflected forms to their lemmas.

sets.title: Finnish passive voice: present vs past
sets.description: Finnish passive forms such as luetaan and luettiin background the actor and focus on the action: 'is read / people read' versus 'was read / people read'.

pairs.question: Which sentence describes what is being read or is generally read now?
pairs.explanation: Luetaan is present passive: the action is current or habitual. Luettiin is past passive: the action happened in the past.

pairs.question: Which sentence places the passive action in the past?
pairs.explanation: Luettiin marks past passive, while luetaan marks present passive. The voice is similar, but the tense changes when the action is situated.

sets.title: Finnish negative auxiliary
sets.description: Finnish negation uses a conjugated negative auxiliary: en, et, ei, emme, ette, eivät. The auxiliary carries person/number, while the main verb appears in a non-finite negative stem.

pairs.question: Which sentence uses the first-person singular negative auxiliary?
pairs.explanation: En is the first-person singular negative auxiliary. Et is second-person singular. Finnish marks the person of negation on the auxiliary rather than on the lexical verb.

pairs.question: Which sentence means 'we do not read'?
pairs.explanation: Emme is first-person plural; ette is second-person plural. The main verb lue stays the same in both forms.

pairs.question: Which sentence uses the plural negative auxiliary?
pairs.explanation: Ei is the third-person singular negative auxiliary. Eivät is third-person plural. The subject number changes the auxiliary, not the main verb form lue.
